tasks.title: Audit and catalog 3D model asset coverage for all 270 base items
tasks.description: We have 270 base items with 106 unique objectRoles, but only 28 Polyhaven prop models and 8 quest-object models (36 total). Survey the gap: (1) Read the full base items list from the DB (isBase: true) and group by objectRole. (2) Read data/base-asset-collections.json to see which objectRoles have Polyhaven mappings. (3) Read client/public/assets/models/props/polyhaven/ and quest-objects/ to verify which .gltf/.glb files actually exist on disk. (4) Produce a gap report listing every objectRole that lacks a real 3D asset. (5) Categorize the gaps by priority: quest-critical items (items referenced by quest objectives) are highest priority, followed by merchant inventory items, then world decoration items. (6) Write the gap report to docs/ASSET_GAP_REPORT.md for reference by subsequent tasks.

tasks.title: Rename quest-objects directory to containers and reorganize asset directories
tasks.description: The current props/ and quest-objects/ directories don't have clear semantic boundaries. Reorganize: (1) Rename client/public/assets/models/quest-objects/ to client/public/assets/models/containers/. Move chest.glb, treasure_chest.gltf, and any barrel/crate models there. (2) Keep client/public/assets/models/props/ for player-interactable items (pickups, tools, weapons, food, etc.). (3) Move avocado_collectible.glb, collectible_gem.glb, water_bottle.glb, brass_lamp.gltf to props/ (they're pickup items, not containers). (4) Keep quest_marker.glb and lantern_marker.gltf in a new client/public/assets/models/markers/ directory since those are UI indicators, not world items. (5) Update ALL import paths across the codebase — search for 'quest-objects' in all .ts files and update to the new paths. (6) Update data/base-asset-collections.json if it references quest-objects paths. (7) Update ProceduralQuestObjects.ts, QuestObjectManager.ts, BabylonGame.ts, and any other files that load from these directories.

tasks.title: Source or create placeholder 3D assets for all uncovered objectRoles
tasks.description: Using the gap report from task 1, create or source placeholder assets for every objectRole that lacks a 3D model. (1) For each missing objectRole, check if Polyhaven (polyhaven.com) has a suitable free CC0 model — download and add to client/public/assets/models/props/polyhaven/. (2) For roles without a Polyhaven match, create simple procedural placeholder .glb files using a script (e.g., colored primitives with labels): food items = brown/green spheres, weapons = gray elongated shapes, tools = brown cylinders, consumables = small colored bottles, materials = cubes with material-appropriate colors, collectibles = shiny spheres, armor = flat shield shapes, keys = yellow key shapes. (3) Place all new assets in the appropriate directory (props/ or containers/). (4) Update data/base-asset-collections.json to map each objectRole to its new asset. (5) Ensure the asset collection resolver in server/migrations/002-seed-base-collections.ts can find them. (6) Target: 100% objectRole coverage — every one of the 106 unique roles should have at least a placeholder model.

tasks.title: Generate Prolog content for Arrival Assessment and Departure Assessment quests
tasks.description: Both assessment quests currently have hasProlog: false, showing 'No Prolog content generated yet' in the editor. Fix this: (1) Read the Arrival Assessment quest structure from shared/assessment/arrival-encounter.ts — it has 5 phases (reading, writing, listening, initiate_conversation, conversation). (2) Read the Departure Assessment from shared/assessment/departure-encounter.ts — 4 phases. (3) Both currently use objective type 'complete_conversation' for all objectives, which doesn't capture the multi-phase nature. Update the assessment-quest-bridge to generate proper objectives with location targets. (4) Write a convertAssessmentToProlog() function in shared/prolog/ that generates Prolog content for assessment quests, similar to convertQuestToProlog() but handling the assessment phase structure. (5) The Arrival Assessment should have its first objective point to the Notice Board location (a settlement landmark). (6) Run the conversion on all assessment quests in worldId 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f. (7) Verify the content field is populated in MongoDB after conversion.

tasks.title: Add locationPosition to all quests that reference specific locations
tasks.description: Every quest in world 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f has hasLocation: false (locationPosition is null). This means NO quest has a minimap marker. Fix this: (1) For quests with visit_location or discover_location objectives targeting 'Coteau-Bas', look up the settlement's world position from the settlements collection and set it as locationPosition. (2) For quests with talk_to_npc objectives, find the assigned NPC's current position or home location and set that as locationPosition. (3) For quests with collect_item objectives, set locationPosition to the area where items are spawned. (4) For conversation-only quests (no specific NPC target), set locationPosition to the nearest NPC cluster or town square. (5) For assessment quests: Arrival should point to the Notice Board (first settlement center), Departure should point to the exit/transit area. (6) Update quest-seed-generator.ts to always compute and set locationPosition when generating quests — use settlement positions from the world data. (7) Run a migration/script to backfill locationPosition for all existing quests in this world.

tasks.title: Implement quest-driven minimap markers that derive from quest objective data
tasks.description: Replace the ad-hoc isFirstPlaythrough/isLanguageLearningWorld check in tryLaunchOnboarding() with a general system where quest markers come from quest data itself. (1) In BabylonGame.ts updateMinimapOverlay(), already collects quest locations from this.quests — enhance this to also pull from individual objective data (objective.locationPosition, objective.target mapped to world positions). (2) For the Arrival Assessment specifically, remove the hardcoded exclamation marker in tryLaunchOnboarding() and instead ensure the quest's locationPosition points to the Notice Board. (3) Add objective-type-specific marker icons: visit_location = cyan diamond, talk_to_npc = green circle, collect_item = gold circle, order_food/haggle_price = orange circle (commercial). (4) For multi-objective quests, show markers for all incomplete objectives simultaneously. (5) Add a 'tracked quest' concept — the player can select which quest to track, and that quest's markers are brighter/larger on the minimap. (6) Ensure markers update dynamically as objectives are completed. (7) Keep the exclamation marker type in BabylonMinimap for special quests (assessment, main story) but trigger it from quest tags rather than ad-hoc code.

tasks.title: Implement dynamic quest waypoints that point toward objective targets
tasks.description: Extend QuestWaypointManager.ts to create in-world directional indicators for active quest objectives. (1) For the currently tracked quest, create a 3D waypoint beam at each incomplete objective's target location using the existing createWaypointForObjectiveType() method. (2) For objectives without a fixed location (e.g., 'talk to any NPC'), create a waypoint pointing to the nearest valid NPC that can fulfill the objective. (3) For objectives targeting businesses (order_food → Restaurant, haggle_price → Shop), create waypoints at the business entrance. (4) Add a compass-style directional indicator at the top of the screen showing the direction and distance to the next objective. (5) Waypoints should fade in/out based on distance (visible from far, hidden when very close). (6) When an objective is completed, smoothly transition the waypoint to the next incomplete objective. (7) For 'conversation-only' quests, the waypoint should point to any nearby NPC since the quest can be completed through any conversation.

tasks.title: Comprehensive quest completability audit for world 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f
tasks.description: Systematically audit every quest to determine if it can actually be completed. There are 52 quests total (50 seed + 2 assessment). For each quest, verify the required game systems exist: (1) COMPLETABLE — conversation-only quests (talk_to_npc, complete_conversation, introduce_self, use_vocabulary, build_friendship, listen_and_repeat, pronunciation_check, translation_challenge, listening_comprehension, write_response, describe_scene, ask_for_directions, order_food, haggle_price): these should work since they just need NPC conversations — verify conversation tracking is wired up for each type. (2) LIKELY BLOCKED — item quests (collect_item, deliver_item, give_gift, craft_item): verify items are spawned in the world and can be picked up; verify delivery targets exist; verify crafting stations exist. (3) LIKELY BLOCKED — world interaction quests (identify_object, examine_object, point_and_name, read_sign, collect_vocabulary): verify interactable objects exist in the world with French labels. (4) LIKELY BLOCKED — navigation quests (visit_location, discover_location, navigate_language, follow_directions): verify settlement positions are resolvable. (5) BLOCKED — combat/escort (defeat_enemies, escort_npc): verify enemy spawning and escort pathfinding. (6) BLOCKED — reputation (gain_reputation): verify reputation system exists. Write the audit results to docs/QUEST_COMPLETABILITY_AUDIT.md with status (COMPLETABLE/BLOCKED/PARTIAL) and specific blockers for each quest.

tasks.title: Fix visit_location and discover_location objective completion detection
tasks.description: Navigation quests (Explore the Neighborhood, Grand Tour, Uncharted Territory, Cultural Landmarks, Newcomer's Welcome, The Full Experience, Language Explorer, Follow the Signs, Direction Master) all target 'Coteau-Bas' but have no locationPosition. Fix: (1) In QuestObjectManager.ts parseQuestObjectives(), for visit_location and discover_location types, resolve the target settlement name to a world position by querying the settlement data. (2) In QuestObjectManager.ts spawnLocationMarker(), use the resolved position to create the cyan beacon. (3) In the game loop, detect when the player enters the settlement radius (e.g., within 50 units of settlement center) and mark the objective complete. (4) For discover_location, additionally check that the player hasn't been there before (track visited locations in playthrough state). (5) For navigate_language objectives, use the navigationWaypoints array to create sequential waypoint markers. (6) For follow_directions objectives, use the directionSteps array to create step-by-step markers. (7) Ensure all these fire the appropriate quest events on the GameEventBus.

tasks.title: Wire collect_item, deliver_item, and give_gift objectives to the inventory system
tasks.description: Item-based quests (A Thoughtful Gift, Scavenger Hunt: Collector, Gather Supplies, Special Delivery) require items to exist in the world and be collectable. (1) In QuestObjectManager.ts, for collect_item objectives, spawn physical item meshes in the world at designated locations (near settlements, in buildings). Use the base item system's objectRole to load the correct 3D model. (2) Wire the item pickup mechanic: when player walks near a quest item and presses E/clicks, add it to BabylonInventory and increment the objective's currentCount. (3) For deliver_item objectives, show a waypoint pointing to the target NPC; when player talks to that NPC while holding the item, mark delivered and remove from inventory. (4) For give_gift objectives, add a 'Give Gift' option to the NPC chat panel when the player has a gift item in inventory. (5) For craft_item (First Craft quest), implement a basic crafting interaction at designated workbenches inside businesses — player selects recipe, materials consumed, new item created. (6) Ensure all these actions fire events on GameEventBus for quest tracking.

tasks.title: Wire identify_object, examine_object, point_and_name, read_sign to world objects
tasks.description: Visual vocabulary quests (Name That Thing, Curious Observer, Point and Say, Reading Around Town, Scavenger Hunt: Basics/Expert) require interactable labeled objects in the world. (1) Ensure world props spawned by the procedural generator have French-language labels (the target language). Each prop's objectRole should map to a base item which has languageLearningData with French translations. (2) For identify_object: when player clicks an object, show a text input prompt asking 'What is this called in French?'; compare input against the item's French name; on success, mark objective progress. (3) For examine_object: when player presses X near an object, show an info panel with the French name, pronunciation, and English translation; mark objective progress. (4) For point_and_name: when player clicks an object (point) then types/speaks the French name, compare and award credit. (5) For read_sign: generate French-language signs near businesses and landmarks; when player approaches, show readable text; mark objective on reading. (6) For collect_vocabulary: similar to examine_object but explicitly adds the word to the player's vocabulary journal. (7) All interactions should fire vocabulary_learned/object_examined events for quest tracking.

tasks.title: Implement persistent NPC interior assignments for businesses
tasks.description: Some quests require interacting with NPCs inside businesses (order_food at Restaurant, haggle_price at Shop). Currently InteriorNPCManager positions NPCs when the player enters, but there's no persistent NPC-to-business assignment. Fix: (1) When a world is generated and NPCs are assigned occupations, store a workBuildingId on each NPC character record linking them to their workplace business. Use the existing occupation/role field to determine which business type they work at. (2) In InteriorNPCManager.ts, when the player enters a building, always show the assigned worker NPCs (not random ones). (3) Add a 'semi-permanent' NPC mode: certain NPCs (shopkeepers, innkeepers, restaurant workers) are always considered to be at their workplace during business hours (8am-8pm game time), even if the full schedule system isn't working. (4) When the player enters a Restaurant, the cook/waiter NPC should be there and interactable. When entering a Shop, the shopkeeper should be there. (5) Expose the NPC's business role in their greeting: 'Welcome to [Business Name]! How can I help you?' (6) Ensure these interior NPCs are registered with the quest system so talk_to_npc objectives targeting business NPCs can be fulfilled.

tasks.title: Ensure quest location markers work correctly with building interiors
tasks.description: When a quest objective points to a location inside a building, the marker system needs to handle the transition. (1) For quests targeting businesses (order_food → Restaurant, haggle_price → Shop), the minimap marker should point to the building entrance in the exterior world. (2) When the player enters the building, the marker should disappear from the exterior minimap (since the player is now inside). (3) Inside the building, if there's a specific NPC to talk to, highlight that NPC with a quest indicator (! or diamond). (4) When the player exits the building, restore exterior markers if the objective isn't complete. (5) Handle the Y-offset issue: interiors are generated at Y=500+, so marker positions need coordinate translation. (6) Add building name labels on the minimap near business buildings that are quest targets, so the player can identify which building to enter. (7) Test with the 'Lunch Order' quest: marker should point to a Restaurant building, and inside, the NPC should have a quest indicator.

tasks.title: Populate building interiors with appropriate item props
tasks.description: Building interiors currently have furniture but lack item props that make them feel lived-in and support item-based quests. (1) In BuildingInteriorGenerator.ts, after generating furniture, spawn contextually appropriate item props: Restaurant → plates, cups, food items on tables; Shop → merchandise items on shelves and counters; Bakery → bread, pastries on display; Pharmacy → bottles, medicine on shelves; Inn/Hotel → lanterns, books, mugs; Residence → personal items, books, decorations. (2) Use the base item system — for each business type, select 3-8 items whose category matches (food for restaurants, consumables for pharmacy, etc.). (3) Load the correct 3D model via the objectRole → asset mapping. (4) Place items on furniture surfaces (table tops, shelf positions, counter tops) using furniture bounding boxes. (5) Make some items interactable (examinable for vocabulary quests, pickable for collect_item quests). (6) Items placed in interiors should be registered with the world item system so they can be quest targets.

tasks.title: Implement a Container system for chests, cupboards, and storage objects
tasks.description: Implement interactive containers that hold items. (1) Define a Container interface in shared/game-engine/types.ts: { id, name, containerType ('chest'|'cupboard'|'barrel'|'crate'|'shelf'|'cabinet'), items: InventoryItem[], capacity: number, isLocked: boolean, position: Vector3, buildingId?: string }. (2) Add a 'containers' collection to the MongoDB schema in shared/schema.ts. (3) Add CRUD endpoints in server/routes.ts: GET/POST/PUT /api/worlds/:worldId/containers, plus GET /api/containers/:id/items. (4) Create a ContainerManager class in client/src/components/3DGame/ContainerManager.ts that tracks container meshes in the world. (5) When the player clicks/approaches a container and presses E, open a container UI showing its contents. (6) Use the existing BabylonShopPanel layout as a base — show items in a scrollable grid, with 'Take' and 'Take All' buttons. (7) Add a 'Place Item' button to move items from player inventory into the container. (8) Containers can be placed in building interiors (chests in bedrooms, cupboards in kitchens, crates in storage rooms) and outdoors (barrels near buildings, crates at market). (9) Load container models from the new containers/ asset directory.

tasks.title: Create container browsing UI panel
tasks.description: Create client/src/components/3DGame/BabylonContainerPanel.ts — a GUI panel for browsing and interacting with container contents. (1) Reuse the layout pattern from BabylonShopPanel.ts (scrollable list, item details, action buttons) but adapt for containers. (2) Show container name and type at the top ('Wooden Chest', 'Kitchen Cupboard'). (3) Display items in a grid: icon, name (with French translation if language-learning world), quantity, rarity indicator. (4) Action buttons per item: 'Take' (moves to player inventory), 'Examine' (shows details, counts for vocabulary objectives). (5) Bulk actions: 'Take All' button. (6) Show a 'Place Item' section at the bottom that shows the player's inventory items that can be placed in the container. (7) Container capacity indicator (e.g., '5/10 slots'). (8) Animate panel open/close similar to inventory and shop panels. (9) Wire container interactions to quest system: taking a specific item from a container can complete collect_item objectives.

tasks.title: Spawn containers in building interiors and outdoor locations
tasks.description: Populate the world with containers. (1) In BuildingInteriorGenerator.ts, spawn containers appropriate to each building type: Residences → 1-2 chests or cupboards containing personal items; Shops → 1-2 crates or shelves with extra merchandise; Restaurants → cupboards with ingredients; Inns → chests in guest rooms. (2) In the outdoor procedural generation, spawn crates and barrels near market areas and building entrances. (3) Fill containers with contextually appropriate items from the base item system. Residence chests: 2-5 random items (tools, collectibles, food). Shop crates: 3-8 items matching the shop's category. (4) Some containers can be quest-specific: place a required quest item in a specific container and set the quest's collect_item objective to reference that container's location. (5) Register all containers with ContainerManager so they're tracked and interactable. (6) Containers should have the appropriate 3D model from the containers/ asset directory (chest.glb, treasure_chest.gltf, or barrel/crate models).

tasks.title: Implement basic mercantile system with player gold and item prices
tasks.description: The shop UI (BabylonShopPanel.ts) and merchant inventory generation (merchant-inventory.ts) already exist but need to be properly wired together with a player economy. (1) Give the player a starting gold amount (e.g., 100g) when a playthrough begins — store in the playthrough record or playerProgress. (2) Ensure all base items have a value field set (many may be 0/null) — run a migration to set sensible prices: common items 5-20g, uncommon 25-75g, rare 100-300g, epic 500-1000g, legendary 2000-5000g. Food/consumables should be cheap (5-30g). (3) Wire BabylonShopPanel's buy/sell actions to actually modify the player's gold: subtract on buy, add on sell (at sellValue, typically 50% of value). (4) Persist gold changes to the server via the player progress API. (5) When the player enters a Shop/Bakery/Restaurant/etc. and talks to the owner NPC, show a 'Browse Wares' button that opens BabylonShopPanel with that merchant's inventory. (6) Verify merchant-inventory.ts generates appropriate stock for each business type. (7) Add a gold display to the HUD (near the minimap or inventory button).

tasks.title: Connect mercantile system to quest objectives
tasks.description: Wire the shop system into quest completion tracking. (1) For order_food objectives (Lunch Order, Dinner Party quests): when the player buys a food item from a Restaurant merchant, count it as 'ordering food' and increment the objective. The conversation around ordering should happen in French. (2) For haggle_price objectives (Bargain Hunter quest): when the player successfully negotiates a lower price (BabylonShopPanel already has language-learning fuzzy-match pricing), count it as haggling and complete the objective. (3) For collect_item objectives that reference purchasable items: buying the item from a merchant should count as 'collecting' it. (4) For give_gift objectives: items bought from merchants should be usable as gifts. (5) Fire purchase/sale events on GameEventBus with item details so the quest system can track them. (6) Add a 'Quest Item Available' indicator in the shop UI when a merchant sells an item needed for an active quest.

tasks.title: Implement defeat_enemies and escort_npc quest mechanics (or mark as unavailable)
tasks.description: Two quests use combat/escort mechanics that may not be implemented: 'Prove Your Mettle' (defeat_enemies) and 'Safe Passage' (escort_npc). Evaluate and implement: (1) For defeat_enemies: check if any combat system exists in the game. If not, either implement a basic combat encounter (enemy spawns near a quest location, player presses F to attack, health bars, simple AI) OR re-tag this quest as 'unavailable' for language-learning worlds and provide an alternative vocabulary/conversation quest. (2) For escort_npc: check if NPC following/pathfinding works. If not, either implement basic escort mechanics (NPC follows player along street network, arrives at destination = complete) OR re-tag as 'unavailable'. (3) For language-learning contexts, combat and escort are low priority — if implementation is complex, mark these quests as hidden/unavailable for language-learning world types and add a note explaining why. (4) If implementing, ensure quest events fire properly for completion tracking.

tasks.title: Implement gain_reputation objective tracking
tasks.description: The 'Earn Their Trust' quest requires gain_reputation but there's no clear reputation system. Implement: (1) Add a simple reputation score per settlement or faction to the player's progress data (e.g., reputation: { 'Coteau-Bas': 0 }). (2) Reputation increases from: completing quests (+5), having conversations (+1), buying from merchants (+2), giving gifts (+3), helping NPCs (+5). (3) In QuestCompletionEngine, track reputation-granting events and check gain_reputation objectives against the accumulated reputation. (4) Define reputation thresholds: 0 = stranger, 10 = acquaintance, 25 = friend, 50 = trusted, 100 = honored. (5) The 'Earn Their Trust' quest should complete when reputation reaches a threshold (e.g., 10 = acquaintance). (6) Show reputation changes as small toast notifications: '+2 Reputation with Coteau-Bas'. (7) NPCs can reference reputation level in their greetings.

tasks.title: Ensure all conversation-only quest objectives have proper completion wiring
tasks.description: Verify that ALL conversation-based objective types actually complete when the player does the required action. For world 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f, these quests are theoretically completable but need verification: (1) talk_to_npc (Introduce Yourself, Meet the Locals, Cultural Exchange): verify GameEventBus fires 'npc_talked' with NPC id, and QuestCompletionEngine increments count. (2) complete_conversation (A Good Chat, Deep Conversation, Grammar in Practice, Tell Your Story, Campfire Tales): verify conversation turn counting works and fires 'conversation_completed' at threshold. (3) introduce_self (First Impressions): verify the introduction detection fires 'introduced_self'. (4) build_friendship (Making Friends): verify repeated conversations with same NPC are tracked. (5) use_vocabulary (Words in Action, Vocabulary Immersion): verify French word detection in player messages increments count. (6) listen_and_repeat (Parrot Practice, Echo Challenge): verify STT comparison completes objectives. (7) pronunciation_check (Say It Right, Fluency Drill): verify pronunciation scoring triggers completion. (8) translation_challenge (Lost in Translation, Master Translator): verify translation UI exists and works. (9) listening_comprehension (Story Time): verify listening UI works. (10) order_food (Lunch Order, Dinner Party): verify food ordering in Restaurant context works. (11) haggle_price (Bargain Hunter): verify price negotiation works. (12) write_response (Written Word): verify writing prompt UI exists. (13) describe_scene (Picture This): verify scene description UI exists. (14) ask_for_directions (Which Way?): verify direction-asking in conversation works. For any that fail, file specific sub-issues and implement the missing wiring.

tasks.title: Add quest-giving NPC indicators and accept/decline flow
tasks.description: Quests should feel like they come from NPCs, not just appear in a list. (1) QuestIndicatorManager.ts already shows !/? marks over NPCs — verify this is wired up: NPCs assigned as quest givers (assignedByCharacterId) should show '!' when they have an available quest, '?' when a quest is ready to turn in. (2) When the player talks to a quest-giving NPC, the NPC should mention the quest in conversation: 'I have a task for you...' and present the quest details. (3) Add Accept/Decline buttons to the chat panel when a quest is offered. (4) On accept, quest status changes from 'available' to 'active' and objectives begin tracking. (5) On decline, quest remains available for later. (6) For seed quests without a specific assignedByCharacterId, distribute them among NPCs based on relevance (merchants give commerce quests, elders give cultural quests, etc.). (7) For assessment quests, the Notice Board itself is the quest giver — show '!' on the Notice Board mesh.

tasks.title: Implement quest completion rewards and turn-in flow
tasks.description: When all objectives are complete, the quest needs a satisfying completion flow. (1) When all objectives of a quest are marked complete, show a 'Quest Complete!' notification with the quest title and XP earned. (2) If the quest has an assignedByCharacterId, add a 'Return to [NPC]' objective that points the player back to the quest giver for turn-in. (3) On turn-in, the NPC congratulates the player in the target language, and rewards are granted: XP from experienceReward, fluency from rewards.fluency, gold from rewards.gold (if any), items from rewards.items (if any). (4) Update quest status to 'completed' on the server via PUT /api/quests/:id. (5) Fire quest_completed event on GameEventBus with full quest data for achievement tracking. (6) For quests without a specific turn-in NPC (e.g., exploration quests), auto-complete and grant rewards immediately when objectives are done. (7) Show a summary panel: quest title, objectives completed, rewards earned, any new quests unlocked.

tasks.title: Populate French-language signs and labels throughout the world
tasks.description: For read_sign and vocabulary quests to work, the world needs visible French text. (1) Generate French-language signs at key locations: business entrances ('Boulangerie', 'Restaurant', 'Pharmacie'), street intersections (street names in French), settlement entrances ('Bienvenue à Coteau-Bas'), landmarks ('Place du Marché'), and directional signs ('Vers le centre-ville →'). (2) Create sign meshes: flat plane with dynamically rendered text using Babylon.js DynamicTexture. (3) Each sign should be registered as an interactable object with objectRole 'sign' and languageLearningData containing the French text and English translation. (4) When the player approaches a sign, show a tooltip with the text. (5) When the player 'reads' the sign (presses X or clicks), show the translation and mark read_sign objective progress. (6) Generate at least 10-15 signs per settlement to provide enough targets for vocabulary quests. (7) Signs should be visible from a reasonable distance and placed at eye level near doorways and intersections.

tasks.title: Populate item spawns in exterior world locations for collection quests
tasks.description: Items need to exist in the outdoor world for collect_item, examine_object, and identify_object quests. (1) Use the item-placement-generator.ts to spawn items in the world — currently it only assigns items to businesses/residences via metadata, but doesn't create 3D representations outdoors. (2) Add an outdoor item spawner in BabylonGame.ts that reads world items with exterior placement and creates pickup-able meshes. (3) Spawn 15-25 items per settlement scattered around: near benches, under trees, beside buildings, at market stalls, along paths. (4) Each item should use its objectRole-mapped 3D model, floating slightly above ground with a gentle bob animation. (5) Items glow or have a particle effect to be visible. (6) When the player approaches and presses E, the item is picked up and added to inventory. (7) Respawn items periodically (e.g., every in-game day) so quests remain completable even if items were already collected by other means. (8) For vocabulary items specifically, show the French name floating above the item.

tasks.title: End-to-end integration test: complete each quest type manually
tasks.description: After all systems are wired up, do a manual integration test for world 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f. For each category of quest, attempt to complete one representative quest end-to-end: (1) Navigation: 'Explore the Neighborhood' — follow marker to Coteau-Bas, arrive, verify visit_location completes. (2) Conversation: 'Introduce Yourself' — find NPC, press G, have conversation, verify talk_to_npc completes. (3) Vocabulary: 'Name That Thing' — find labeled objects, click and type French name, verify identify_object completes. (4) Collection: 'Gather Supplies' — find item in world, pick up, verify collect_item completes. (5) Commerce: 'Lunch Order' — enter Restaurant, talk to NPC, order food, verify order_food completes. (6) Delivery: 'Special Delivery' — collect item, deliver to NPC, verify both objectives complete. (7) Assessment: 'Arrival Assessment' — find Notice Board marker, begin assessment, complete phases. (8) Multi-objective: 'Newcomer's Welcome' — visit location, talk to NPC, collect vocabulary, verify all 3 complete. (9) For any quest that fails, document the specific failure point and create a follow-up fix. (10) Write test results to docs/QUEST_INTEGRATION_TEST_RESULTS.md.
